summary: McKenzie shares practical tips for designing effective landing pages, drawing from his experience redesigning Bingo Card Creator's landing page which increased AdWords conversions from 18% to 28%. He advocates for ruthless focus on a single goal, stripping away all navigation and visual elements that don't serve that goal. The post covers trust markers like testimonials, money-back guarantees, pleasing Google's landing page quality signals, and maintaining continuity between ad copy and landing page messaging. He emphasizes that users skim rather than read, and that the entire flow from ad click to conversion should feel like one continuous experience.
key_points: Landing pages should have exactly one goal — strip out all navigation, eliminate distractions, and make the desired action blazingly obvious
Testimonials work but need editing: bold the selling bits, cut what doesn't sell, while keeping them authentic enough not to seem manufactured
Internet users skim more than they read and feel more than they think, so design for scanning behavior rather than careful reading
Continuity between ad copy and landing page call-to-action reduces friction — visitors arrive mentally invested in the action your ad promised
Google is a shadow audience for your landing pages: include trust markers, avoid looking like an affiliate, and handle duplicate content with noindex tags
money_quotes: Landing pages should have one goal.
I cannot stress enough how much people do not read on the Internet.
They are not professional marketers and should not be expected to write like them. You are, so give them a minor hand.

I don't feel like elaborating on this, other than to say I once walked out the door without pants on. That is a pretty serious oversight, but it still isn't as bad as forgetting your trusty money-back guarantee.
Technically speaking there are more search engines than Google to worry about. Hah. Sorry, where was I.
Seeing your ad, clicking on your ad, reading your landing page, and then interaction with The One True Goal should be one continuous flow, free of jarring transitions and mental static.
Polishing it until it is too good is another danger. Testimonials should remain authentic.
key_insight: A landing page should ruthlessly serve a single goal, with every element — from stripped navigation to edited testimonials to ad-matched copy — aligned to move the visitor toward that one action.
